title: Documenting the Journey as an AI Engineer
excerpt: I graduated from Penn State in May 2025 with a degree in Computational Data Science. Two months later, I started my first role as an AI Engineer. It's been almost a year now, and I've decided to start writing things down.
content:
I graduated from Penn State in May 2025 with a degree in Computational Data Science. Two months later, I started my first role as an AI Engineer. It's been almost a year now, and I've decided to start writing things down.

This blog is where I document what I'm actually learning — the problems I run into, the tools I'm using, and the decisions I make. If you're working with AI or thinking about it, some of this might be useful.

## AI Doing What I Learned in Class — in Seconds

When I started the job, I also started using Claude Code. Things I spent semesters learning in class — Claude Code could do in seconds. Not perfectly every time, but fast enough to make me rethink what my time should be spent on.

The job isn't about writing every line yourself. It's about knowing what to build, understanding the output, and catching the mistakes. The classroom knowledge became the foundation for knowing when the AI is wrong.

## Working With Claude Code

People call it "vibe coding" or agentic engineering, but it's simpler than it sounds. You set the direction, review the output, and iterate. The AI is a collaborator, not a replacement.

You still need to understand the code. You still need to catch bugs and guide the agent when it goes off track. But when the loop works, you move faster than you could alone.

---

*Written with the help of AI*
